Party_Details.Name: Paillart Philippe Marcel Etienne

Facts.Text: Philippe Marcel Etienne Paillart is the managing director of Sin Rong Investments Pte Ltd.
On 28 April 2005, Philippe Marcel Etienne Paillart was voted onto the board of directors of Earth Science Industries Pte Ltd.
On 28 April 2005, the parties entered into a written agreement to enable Sin Rong Investments Pte Ltd to financially invest in Earth Science Industries Pte Ltd.

Clause 5(b) of the Agreement provided that as long as Sin Rong Investments Pte Ltd held at least 10% of the shares in Earth Science Industries Pte Ltd, Philippe Marcel Etienne Paillart would be entitled to be a director.
A machine was fabricated in April 2005 after the plaintiffs contributed nearly $800,000; Eban Stuart Ashley and Michael John Geraghty contributed about $4,500 each.
A directors' resolution dated 6 May 2005 was signed by Philippe Marcel Etienne Paillart to register a company in the Sharjah Airport International Free Zone in the United Arab Emirates.
Philippe Marcel Etienne Paillart signed a power of attorney in favour of his son, Nicholas Paillart, in relation to the UAE registration.
Geraghty submitted an application form dated 4 May 2005 for a SAIF-Zone licence and inserted the name of 'Nicholas Philippe' as the manager of the proposed company.
In a separate application form for registration in the SAIF Zone, Geraghty again inserted the name 'Nicholas Philippe' as the manager.
On 24 June 2005, Eban Stuart Ashley called for the resignation of Philippe Marcel Etienne Paillart, citing the 6 May 2005 resolution and the UAE registration as grounds.
On 27 June 2005, Eban Stuart Ashley issued a notice to call for an extraordinary general meeting of Earth Science Industries Pte Ltd to remove Philippe Marcel Etienne Paillart from the board.
The proposed resolution for the extraordinary general meeting was not circulated to Philippe Marcel Etienne Paillart prior to the notice.
Earth Science Industries Pte Ltd had a balance of $25.75 in its bank account in October 2004.
The intellectual property rights in the company's machine were estimated to be worth between $5m and $7m.
The machine was sold to Oceanic Shiphandlers for 41,210.50 dirhams on 11 September 2005.
Geraghty removed the machine from the warehouse on 13 September 2005.
Eban Stuart Ashley requested a quote to purchase the machine from Oceanic Shiphandlers on 27 December 2005 on behalf of his own company, Kaitiaki Pte Ltd.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether Philippe Marcel Etienne Paillart's removal from the board of Earth Science Industries Pte Ltd was valid in light of the 28 April 2005 agreement and the procedures followed to convene the extraordinary general meeting.
Party_Details.Rule: A director's removal must comply with contractual entitlements held by shareholders and the procedural requirements of the company's constitution, including the proper circulation of resolutions and notice to all directors.
Party_Details.Application: Sin Rong Investments Pte Ltd held shares in Earth Science Industries Pte Ltd under an agreement that entitled Philippe Marcel Etienne Paillart to a board seat while a minimum shareholding was maintained. Following a disagreement regarding UAE-based operations, Eban Stuart Ashley issued a notice to convene a meeting for Paillart's removal. The underlying circular resolution was not shared with Paillart. During the same period, the company's primary asset, a waste-conversion machine, was sold to a third party and subsequently sought to be repurchased by Eban's personal company.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judge analysed whether the default judgment against the second defendant rendered the declaratory relief against the first defendant unnecessary, applying principles from Salijah bte Ab Latef v Mohd Irwan bin Abdullah Teo and authorities on declaratory judgments. He concluded that a real controversy remained between the plaintiffs and the first defendant and that the plaintiffs were entitled to pursue declaratory and injunctive relief despite the default judgment. The court considered the first defendant's conduct in defending the action and raising the res judicata argument only at closing submissions, and treated the argument as a strategic attempt to avoid costs. On the contractual issue, the judge construed clause 3 of the agreement as expressing only an intention to invite Green SA to invest, not an obligation on the first plaintiff to procure the investment, and held that any failure to secure Green SA's investment did not amount to a repudiatory breach. He further held that, even if there had been a repudiatory breach, the defendants had not accepted it and the agreement, including clause 5(b), remained in force. On the alleged gross misconduct, the judge evaluated the evidence regarding the 2005-05-06 resolution and power of attorney, the use of the title "President", the involvement of Nicholas Paillart, and the Free Zone Establishment applications. He accepted the first plaintiff's explanation that the documents required signatures of the other directors and that the wording "we the Directors" indicated that they were not intended to be effective with only his signature. The judge rejected the allegations of fraud and gross misconduct, applying the standard of proof for serious allegations as discussed in Tang Yoke Kheng v Lek Benedict. He found that the first defendant had not proved the alleged misconduct on a balance of probabilities. The judge also examined the events surrounding the machine and its sale to Oceanic Shiphandlers, including the appointment of Geraghty as guardian after his resignation as director, the sale price, and the first defendant's subsequent attempt to purchase the machine through Kaitiaki Pte Ltd. He concluded that the first defendant had acted dishonestly in relation to the machine and that this undermined the first defendant's complaints about the first plaintiff's conduct. On the Companies Act defence, the judge held that, since no misconduct had been established, there was no contravention of section 157, and in any event proper procedures were required for removal. Regarding the notice of the extraordinary general meeting, the judge found that the directors' circular resolution to convene the meeting had not been sent to the first plaintiff and, applying Polybuilding (S) Pte Ltd v Lim Heng Lee, held that a meeting of the majority without notice to the minority was ineffective, rendering the notice of the extraordinary general meeting defective. The judge rejected the argument that the plaintiffs should be denied equitable relief on the basis of unclean hands, reiterating his findings on the lack of proven misconduct by the first plaintiff and the adverse view of the first defendant's conduct. He therefore granted the plaintiffs a declaration that, for as long as the second plaintiff holds at least 10% of the shares of the second defendant, the first plaintiff is entitled to be appointed a director of the second defendant, and a permanent injunction restraining the first defendant from convening or holding meetings or taking steps to remove the first plaintiff as director or to curtail his powers, together with damages to be assessed, interest, liberty to apply, and costs.

Party_Details.Name: Sin Rong Investments Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: Sin Rong Investments Pte Ltd is an investment company incorporated in Singapore.
On 28 April 2005, Sin Rong Investments Pte Ltd entered into an agreement to financially invest in Earth Science Industries Pte Ltd.
Sin Rong Investments Pte Ltd became a shareholder holding 22.22% of the shares in Earth Science Industries Pte Ltd.
The investment agreement provided that as long as Sin Rong Investments Pte Ltd held no less than 10% of the shares, its representative would be entitled to a board seat.
Sin Rong Investments Pte Ltd contributed nearly S$800,000 towards the fabrication of a plantation waste-processing machine.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether Sin Rong Investments Pte Ltd was entitled to maintain its board representation in Earth Science Industries Pte Ltd in accordance with the 28 April 2005 agreement.
Party_Details.Rule: A shareholder's contractual entitlement to board representation must be respected unless the agreement is validly terminated or the representative is removed via lawful corporate procedures.
Party_Details.Application: Sin Rong Investments Pte Ltd acquired a 22.22% stake in Earth Science Industries Pte Ltd and provided the majority of the capital required to develop the company's only valuable asset. A dispute arose involving the other directors that led to an attempt to remove Sin Rong’s representative from the board without notice to that representative.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judge treated Sin Rong Investments Pte Ltd's contractual and shareholding position as central to the relief sought. He interpreted clause 5(b) of the agreement as conferring a continuing entitlement, while the second plaintiff held at least 10% of the shares, for the first plaintiff to be appointed a director. He rejected the first defendant's argument that an alleged breach of clause 3 by the first plaintiff allowed the defendants to treat the agreement as at an end, holding that clause 3 did not impose an obligation to procure Green SA's investment and that, in any event, no repudiation had been accepted. The judge concluded that the agreement remained in force and that Sin Rong Investments Pte Ltd's shareholding and the linked director-entitlement clause continued to operate. He therefore granted a declaration in favour of both plaintiffs that, for as long as Sin Rong Investments Pte Ltd holds at least 10% of the shares of Earth Science Industries Pte Ltd, the first plaintiff is entitled to be appointed a director, and he issued a permanent injunction restraining the first defendant from taking steps to remove the first plaintiff or to curtail his powers as director.

Party_Details.Name: Eban Stuart Ashley

Facts.Text: Eban Stuart Ashley is a director of Earth Science Industries Pte Ltd.
Prior to 28 April 2005, the board of directors of Earth Science Industries Pte Ltd comprised Paillart, Eban, and Geraghty.
Eban Stuart Ashley and Geraghty contributed approximately $4,500 each towards the company's machine in April 2005.
On 24 June 2005, Eban Stuart Ashley demanded the resignation of Paillart based on allegations regarding a UAE-based registration.
On 27 June 2005, Eban Stuart Ashley issued a notice to call for an extraordinary general meeting for the purpose of removing Paillart from the board.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether Eban Stuart Ashley's attempt to remove a co-director was procedurally and contractually valid.
Party_Details.Rule: A director's attempt to remove a board member must align with the company's articles of association and any overriding shareholder agreements, ensuring that all procedural requirements for the convening of meetings are met.
Party_Details.Application: Eban Stuart Ashley initiated removal proceedings against a co-director whose position was protected by an investment agreement. This process involved a circular resolution that was not sent to the targeted director. Simultaneously, Eban was involved in the sale of the company's only valuable asset and a subsequent attempt to repurchase it personally.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judge considered the first defendant's five main defences: alleged breach of the agreement by failure to secure Green SA's investment, alleged gross misconduct by the first plaintiff, alleged contravention of section 157 of the Companies Act, the propriety of convening the extraordinary general meeting, and the contention that equitable relief should be denied due to unclean hands. On the agreement, the judge held that clause 3 expressed only an intention to invite Green SA to invest and did not impose an obligation on the first plaintiff to procure the investment; thus, there was no repudiatory breach. He further held that, even if there had been such a breach, the defendants had not accepted any repudiation, so the agreement, including clause 5(b), remained in force. On the alleged gross misconduct, the judge examined the 2005-05-06 resolution and power of attorney, the use of the title "President", the wording "we the Directors", and the involvement of Nicholas Paillart. Applying the heightened scrutiny appropriate for serious allegations, he concluded that the first defendant had not proved fraud or gross misconduct on a balance of probabilities. The judge also reviewed the events concerning the machine, including the appointment of Geraghty as guardian after his resignation as director, the sale to Oceanic Shiphandlers at 41,210.50 dirhams, and the first defendant's subsequent attempt to purchase the machine through Kaitiaki Pte Ltd. He found that the first defendant had acted dishonestly in relation to the machine and that this undermined the first defendant's position in complaining about the first plaintiff's conduct. On the Companies Act defence, the judge held that, since no misconduct had been established, there was no breach of section 157, and in any event proper procedures were required for removal. Regarding the extraordinary general meeting, the judge found that the directors' circular resolution to convene the meeting had not been sent to the first plaintiff and, relying on Polybuilding (S) Pte Ltd v Lim Heng Lee, held that a meeting of the majority without notice to the minority was ineffective, rendering the notice of the extraordinary general meeting defective. The judge rejected the argument that the plaintiffs should be denied equitable relief due to unclean hands, noting both the failure to prove misconduct by the first plaintiff and the adverse findings about the first defendant's conduct. He also rejected the first defendant's argument that the action was misconceived due to the default judgment against the second defendant, holding that a real controversy remained and that the first defendant, having chosen to defend the action and raise numerous allegations, could not rely on res judicata to avoid the relief and costs consequences. The court therefore granted the declaratory and injunctive relief sought against the first defendant, together with damages to be assessed, interest, liberty to apply, and costs.

Party_Details.Name: Earth Science Industries Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: Earth Science Industries Pte Ltd had a balance of $25.75 in its bank account in October 2004.
Prior to 28 April 2005, Earth Science Industries Pte Ltd was wholly owned by Century Trading Group.
The company's articles allowed resolutions to be passed by a majority vote.
The company was the sole beneficial owner of intellectual property for a plantation waste-processing machine.
The machine was the company's only valuable asset, estimated at $5m to $7m.
Earth Science Industries Pte Ltd was a party to the 28 April 2005 investment agreement involving Paillart and Sin Rong Investments.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the company's board processes and asset disposals were governed by the 28 April 2005 agreement and constitutional procedures.
Party_Details.Rule: Corporate actions, including the removal of directors and the disposal of core assets, must be authorized in accordance with the company's governing documents and any overriding shareholder agreements.
Party_Details.Application: Earth Science Industries Pte Ltd was the vehicle for an investment agreement that linked board representation to shareholding levels. The company's name was used to initiate a director's removal and to sell its primary machine while a default judgment was eventually entered against it for failing to file a defense.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: Default judgment was entered against Earth Science Industries Pte Ltd on 2005-11-11 due to its failure to file a defence. The judge considered whether this default judgment rendered further declaratory relief unnecessary and concluded that it did not, because a real controversy remained between the plaintiffs and the first defendant and because the first defendant could have sought to set aside the default judgment or to carry on the company's defence but did not do so. The judge treated the company's position as governed by the 2005-04-28 agreement and its memorandum and articles, including Article 105. He held that the agreement, including clause 5(b), remained in force and that the company's use as a vehicle for the first defendant's attempt to remove the first plaintiff had to comply with those instruments. The judge found that the directors' circular resolution to convene the extraordinary general meeting had not been sent to the first plaintiff and, applying Polybuilding (S) Pte Ltd v Lim Heng Lee, held that a meeting of the majority without notice to the minority was ineffective, rendering the notice of extraordinary general meeting defective. He also noted the company's ownership of the machine and its intellectual property rights, the sale of the machine to Oceanic Shiphandlers, and the subsequent attempt by the first defendant to reacquire it through Kaitiaki Pte Ltd, and he regarded these events as relevant to assessing the conduct of those acting in the company's name. The court ultimately granted declaratory and injunctive relief that constrained the use of Earth Science Industries Pte Ltd as a vehicle for removing the first plaintiff from its board and ordered damages, interest, liberty to apply, and costs.
